title: CLAUDE.md — IS428 Visual Analytics Project
text:
This file is automatically read by Claude Code at every session start. Always read it and all files in .claude/context/ before making changes.


IS428 Visual Analytics, AY2025-2026 Term 2, Singapore Management University. Topic: VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 1 — Music Knowledge Graph. We are building a Tableau application and a Quarto project website to help journalist Silas Reed analyse the rise of Sailor Shift and the spread of Oceanus Folk music.




Quarto website (this repo) — deployed via Vercel — documents the project
Tableau application (.twbx) — built separately — answers MC1 questions The website shows screenshots of Tableau dashboards and written analysis. The website does NOT embed or run Tableau.





Radim Soukal: Task 3 (Rising Star Prediction) + GitHub & website owner
Klara: Task 2 (Oceanus Folk Genre Spread)
Charles: Task 1 (Sailor Shift Career Profile)





.claude/context/team.md
.claude/context/MC1_task_details.md
.claude/context/dataset_schema.md
.claude/context/design_theme.md





Radim: index.qmd, proposal.qmd, _quarto.yml, CLAUDE.md, styles.css, task3_analysis.qmd, userguide.qmd, poster.qmd, images/task3/
Klara: task2_analysis.qmd, images/task2/
Charles: task1_analysis.qmd, images/task1/





Never use placeholder text
No spaces in image filenames
Always use relative image paths (never starting with /)
After structural changes: quarto render
After any change: remind Radim to git add . && git commit && git push
Vercel auto-deploys on every push to main





Proposal live on Vercel: 18 March 2026 (11:59pm SGT)
Poster to eLearn: 8 April 2026
Poster presentation: 13 April 2026
Final submission: 19 April 2026

title: About
text:
We are a group of three IS428 students from Singapore Management University (SMU), School of Computing and Information Systems.
This website documents our Visual Analytics Project for AY2025-2026 Term 2, based on VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 1. The project applies visual analytics techniques to a music knowledge graph to help journalist Silas Reed tell the story of Sailor Shift’s rise and the spread of Oceanus Folk music.

This project was completed under the guidance of Prof. Kam Tin Seong, Associate Professor of Information Systems (Practice), Singapore Management University. The dataset is sourced from the VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 1, made available for academic use by the IEEE VAST Challenge organising committee.

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
text: This page summarises the analytical findings from all three tasks of the VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 1 investigation into the rise of Sailor Shift and Oceanus Folk music.

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
text: Task 1 — Sailor Shift Career Profile

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
text:
Overview
Task 1 investigates the career trajectory of Sailor Shift — the artist credited with bringing Oceanus Folk to global prominence. Using the VAST Challenge 2025 MC1 knowledge graph, we extracted three focused datasets (task1_sailor_works.csv, task1_all_collaborators.csv, task1_bandmates.csv) to answer three questions: who influenced her, who she collaborated with, and how she in turn influenced the broader Oceanus Folk community. The two interactive network graphs below visualise her direct collaboration circle and her indirect influence chain, constructed from 204 nodes and 362 edges in the full knowledge graph.

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
section: Question A — Who Has She Been Most Influenced By?
text:
Question A — Who Has She Been Most Influenced By?
Sailor Shift’s knowledge graph entries reveal a rich tapestry of inbound influence. Edges of type InStyleOf, InterpolatesFrom, and DirectlySamples pointing toward her works trace stylistic debts to multiple genres — predominantly Indie Folk and Indie Pop, alongside early Oceanus Folk pioneers. Her earliest recorded works already show interpolation from established Oceanus Folk acts, suggesting she absorbed and synthesised the genre rather than inventing it in isolation. With 38 distinct works attributed to her in the graph, the breadth of these inbound influence edges signals an artist who consciously drew from a wide palette before developing her own signature sound. Notably, the density of these edges increases around her mid-career period, consistent with a period of intensive stylistic experimentation before her commercial breakthrough.

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
section: Question B — Who Has She Collaborated With and Influenced?
text:
Question B — Who Has She Collaborated With and Influenced?

Direct Collaborators
Sailor Shift has 43 direct collaborators recorded in the knowledge graph, spanning roles including composer, lyricist, performer, and producer. These collaborators co-created the 38 works attributed to her, with many individuals contributing in multiple capacities across several projects. This breadth is consistent with the journalistic accounts of Sailor actively seeking out lesser-known artists and providing them with a platform — her collaboration network is unusually wide for an artist of her career stage, with relatively few repeat collaborators, suggesting deliberate outreach rather than a tight inner circle. Her four Ivy Echoes bandmates (captured separately as Ivy Echoes Member nodes) form the densest sub-cluster within this network, having co-created her earliest and most genre-defining works.


Indirect Influence
Beyond direct collaboration, the knowledge graph captures 28 works made explicitly InStyleOf Sailor or her circle, connected to a further 22 nodes in the “Inspired By Sailor Circle” category. This second-degree influence chain extends outward from her direct works into the broader Oceanus Folk community and beyond — genres including Indie Rock, Synthwave, and Americana all show downstream influence edges originating from Sailor’s catalogue. The directed influence network below makes this chain navigable: follow any orange Sailor Work node outward to see which later works interpolated or stylistically referenced it, and which artists those works belong to.

  Direct Collaborators Network
  
    Interactive — drag nodes to explore. Gold node = Sailor Shift.
    Blue = direct collaborators. Teal = Ivy Echoes members.
    Hover an edge to see the collaboration role (LyricistOf, ComposerOf, etc.).
  
  
  



  Direct and Indirect Influence Network
  
    Interactive — drag nodes to explore. Gold star = Sailor Shift.
    Orange = her works. Blue = direct collaborators. Teal = Ivy Echoes members.
    Purple = works inspired by her circle. Hover any node for genre and release year.

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction
section: Question C — How Has She Influenced the Oceanus Folk Community?
text:
Question C — How Has She Influenced the Oceanus Folk Community?
Sailor Shift’s four Ivy Echoes bandmates — Maya Jensen, Lila Hartman, Jade Thompson, and Sophie Ramirez — each continued active careers after the band’s dissolution, and each contributed independently to Oceanus Folk’s ongoing spread. Sophie Ramirez transitioned into music production, directly enabling a new cohort of artists to record and release within the Oceanus Folk tradition. The others continued as performers and songwriters, carrying the genre’s identifying harmonic and lyrical conventions into new collaborations. Taken together, the Ivy Echoes alumni function as a secondary diffusion layer: Sailor Shift’s influence does not terminate at her direct collaborators but propagates further through the bandmates’ own networks, accelerating the genre’s reach beyond what any single artist’s catalogue could achieve alone.

title: Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction

title: Poster

Poster

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text: Team: Radim Šoukal ([01550535])  ·  [Klara Kasparova] ([01550536])  ·  [Charles-Antoine Tremblay] ([01550160])

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text:
Context
Oceanus, a small island nation once known primarily for its fishing communities, produced one of the most unlikely stories in modern music history. Sailor Shift — a vocalist who began her career in the niche Oceanus Folk genre — rose from regional obscurity to global recognition over the course of two decades. In 2023 she joined the Ivy Echoes, an all-female Oceanus Folk band, before her 2028 breakthrough single became the first Oceanus Folk song ever to top global charts. By 2040, journalist Silas Reed is writing a long-form article, “Oceanus Folk: Then and Now”, tracing this transformation and its lasting impact on the music world. To support his reporting, he has assembled a knowledge graph of artists, songs, albums, labels, and the influence relationships between them — and he needs visual analytics tools to make sense of it.
The MC1 knowledge graph contains 17,412 nodes across five types (Person, Song, Album, RecordLabel, MusicalGroup) and 37,857 directed edges across twelve relationship types, spanning nearly five decades from 1993 to 2040. This scale and relational complexity makes the dataset impossible to interpret through manual exploration or conventional tabular analysis. Purpose-built interactive visualisations are essential to surface the patterns of influence, collaboration, and genre evolution that Silas needs for his article.

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text:
Motivation & Objectives

Motivation
The fundamental challenge Silas Reed faces is one of scale and relational complexity. A knowledge graph with nearly 18,000 nodes and 38,000 edges cannot be meaningfully explored through tabular tools or manual browsing. At this size, patterns that span dozens or hundreds of relationships — such as how an artist’s stylistic choices propagated through a generation of subsequent musicians — are simply invisible without automated aggregation and visual encoding. The graph’s five node types and twelve edge types create a multi-relational structure where the same artist node may participate simultaneously in performer, composer, lyricist, producer, and influence relationships, each of which carries different analytical weight depending on the question being asked.
Beyond scale, the data has a temporal dimension that spans from 1993 to 2040 — nearly five decades — making longitudinal analysis essential. Musical influence is not static: artists who were obscure in one decade may become central nodes of influence in the next, and genres that once absorbed influence from Oceanus Folk may later feed inspiration back into it. Standard visualisation tools designed for snapshot data cannot capture this bidirectional, time-varying flow of influence. Finally, the multi-hop nature of influence propagation — where artist A shapes artist B, who in turn shapes artist C — requires graph traversal visualisation techniques that go beyond the capabilities of conventional business intelligence software. These three factors together make this project an ideal candidate for visual analytics.


Objectives

Task 1 — Sailor Shift Career Profile (Charles): Profile Sailor Shift’s career through interactive Tableau visualisations that map who influenced her and when, who she has collaborated with, and which artists she directly and indirectly shaped — tracing her impact on the broader Oceanus Folk community over time.
Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread (Klara): Trace the spread of Oceanus Folk as a genre by visualising how its influence expanded into adjacent genres such as Indie Folk, Dream Pop, Desert Rock, and Space Rock, whether that expansion was gradual or episodic, and how the genre itself has evolved by absorbing external influences since Sailor’s breakthrough.
Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction (Radim): Develop a data-driven profile of what characterises a breakout Oceanus Folk artist by examining the career trajectories of established rising stars, compare candidate artists against this benchmark, and name three evidence-based predictions for the next Oceanus Folk stars likely to emerge over the next five years.

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text:
Methodology
This project translates the MC1 knowledge graph into a suite of interactive Tableau dashboards, one per task, integrated into a single combined workbook. The raw graph data (MC1_graph.json) was first converted into flat CSV tables — separating node types and edge types — suitable for Tableau’s relational data model. Each sub-module then builds its visualisations on top of this prepared data.

Task 1 (Charles): Four interconnected dashboards — an influence timeline showing who shaped Sailor and when, a collaboration network graph centred on Sailor as the hub node, an indirect influence tree tracing second- and third-degree impacts, and a career tracker for her Ivy Echoes bandmates.
Task 2 (Klara): A Sankey-style genre flow diagram showing Oceanus Folk’s outward influence over time, a genre influence timeline chart from 1993 to 2040, a ranked bar chart of top influenced artists, and a genre inspiration heatmap showing which external genres have fed back into Oceanus Folk.
Task 3 (Radim): A radar chart defining the rising star profile across multiple metrics, a multi-line career arc comparison for three benchmark artists, a ranked candidate list with visual supporting evidence, and a prediction dashboard comparing top candidates against the rising star benchmark.

title: Oceanus Folk: Then and Now
text:
Find Out More
Read our full Project Proposal for detailed methodology, data description, and project timeline.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Sailor Shift is an Oceanus Folk artist who co-founded the Ivy Echoes in 2023 before achieving a viral breakthrough in 2028 — the first Oceanus Folk artist to top global charts. Since then, she has released acclaimed albums annually, collaborated widely across genres, and actively mentored emerging artists, while her former bandmates have independently contributed to the spread of Oceanus Folk across the music world.
Understanding this network of influence is the core challenge. Journalist Silas Reed has compiled a knowledge graph of 17,412 nodes and 37,857 edges spanning music history from 1993 to 2040. While this dataset contains all the answers needed for his article “Oceanus Folk: Then-and-Now”, raw graph data is unreadable without purpose-built visualisation. Tracing Sailor’s influences, measuring how Oceanus Folk spread into other genres, or identifying the next rising star cannot be done by browsing a JSON file.
By transforming this knowledge graph into interactive Tableau dashboards, we turn a complex relational dataset into clear, navigable insights about how musical influence works and how stars are made.




Task 1 — Career Profile: Design Tableau visualisations to map Sailor Shift’s influences, collaborations, and the direct and indirect impact she has had on other artists and on the Oceanus Folk community. This includes tracing who shaped her sound and when, identifying her key collaborators, and visualising the downstream influence chain.
Task 2 — Genre Spread: Develop visualisations showing how Oceanus Folk’s influence has spread across the music world since Sailor’s breakthrough, which genres it most affected (Indie Folk, Dream Pop, Desert Rock, Space Rock, Synthwave, etc.), and how the genre itself has evolved through external influences, making visible the flow between Oceanus Folk and the broader musical ecosystem.
Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction: Build a data-driven profile of what constitutes a breakout Oceanus Folk artist, compare the career trajectories of three benchmark artists, and provide three predictions for the next stars likely to emerge over the next five years, drawing on the notable works and notoriety date data as the primary signal of an artist’s rising trajectory.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Sailor Shift is an Oceanus Folk artist who co-founded the Ivy Echoes in 2023 before achieving a viral breakthrough in 2028 — the first Oceanus Folk artist to top global charts. Since then, she has released acclaimed albums annually, collaborated widely across genres, and actively mentored emerging artists, while her former bandmates have independently contributed to the spread of Oceanus Folk across the music world.
Understanding this network of influence is the core challenge. Journalist Silas Reed has compiled a knowledge graph of 17,412 nodes and 37,857 edges spanning music history from 1993 to 2040. While this dataset contains all the answers needed for his article “Oceanus Folk: Then-and-Now”, raw graph data is unreadable without purpose-built visualisation. Tracing Sailor’s influences, measuring how Oceanus Folk spread into other genres, or identifying the next rising star cannot be done by browsing a JSON file.
By transforming this knowledge graph into interactive Tableau dashboards, we turn a complex relational dataset into clear, navigable insights about how musical influence works and how stars are made.




Task 1 — Career Profile: Design Tableau visualisations to map Sailor Shift’s influences, collaborations, and the direct and indirect impact she has had on other artists and on the Oceanus Folk community. This includes tracing who shaped her sound and when, identifying her key collaborators, and visualising the downstream influence chain.

Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction: Build a data-driven profile of what constitutes a breakout Oceanus Folk artist, compare the career trajectories of three benchmark artists, and provide three predictions for the next stars likely to emerge over the next five years, drawing on the notable works and notoriety date data as the primary signal of an artist’s rising trajectory.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Data
The datasets for this project are mc1_nodes.csv and mc1_edges.csv, derived from MC1_graph.json provided by VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 1. They represent the music industry in and around Oceanus from 1993 to 2040, containing 17,412 nodes and 37,857 edges across five node types and twelve relationship types. Edge types capture creative relationships including performance, composition, production, and stylistic influence — with the influence category (InStyleOf, InterpolatesFrom, CoverOf, DirectlySamples) forming the primary analytical layer for tracing how musical ideas spread between artists and genres.
Two fields are critical for Task 3. The notable boolean records whether a work charted, and notoriety_date records when — together allowing reconstruction of each artist’s breakthrough trajectory across 649 charted works.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Scope of Work

Data preparation: parsing mc1_edges.csv and mc1_nodes.csv into twelve task-specific CSVs via Python, with NA handling, date normalisation, and name resolution across source/target edge endpoints
Task 1 — Sailor Shift Career Profile (Charles): influence timeline, collaboration network graph, indirect influence tree, and Ivy Echoes bandmate career tracker in Tableau
Task 2 — Oceanus Folk Genre Spread (Klara): Sankey genre flow diagram, influence timeline chart, top influenced artists bar chart, and genre inspiration heatmap in Tableau
Task 3 — Rising Star Prediction (Radim): rising star radar chart, career arc comparison across three artists, and breakout candidate dashboard with three evidence-based predictions in Tableau
Integration: combining all sub-modules into a single .twbx file with cross-dashboard filter actions
Website: Quarto project website deployed via Vercel, with individual analysis pages and screenshots per sub-module
Poster: ISO A1 format (594 × 841mm, 300dpi JPEG) submitted to eLearn by 8 April 2026
User guide: step-by-step documentation of all Tableau dashboard interactions
